Classification of Möbius Transformations

A fundamental way to understand linear fractional (Möbius) transformations
          is through their fixed points. A point @@math:118:1c5ddf@@ is called a [[em]]fixed point[[/em]]
          of a transformation @@math:119:e42f64@@ if
          @@math:120:306011@@
          For a transformation
          @@math:121:09de4e@@
          the fixed points are obtained by solving
          @@math:122:d4a359@@
          which leads to the quadratic equation
          @@math:123:a556cb@@

Thus, a Möbius transformation has either one or two fixed points
          in the extended complex plane. This observation leads to a natural classification.
1. Transformations with two distinct fixed points

If the quadratic equation has two distinct solutions, then the transformation
          has two distinct fixed points. In this case, the transformation is conjugate
          to a simple dilation or rotation of the form
          @@math:124:e54486@@
If @@math:125:d1078e@@, the transformation is called a [[em]]hyperbolic transformation[[/em]].

If @@math:126:1311b4@@ (but @@math:127:23705c@@ to avoid the identity), the transformation is called an [[em]]elliptic
              transformation[[/em]].

Geometrically, these transformations preserve the set of circles and lines
          and act as scalings or rotations after a suitable change of coordinates.
2. Transformations with one (double) fixed point

If the quadratic equation has a repeated root, then the transformation has
          exactly one fixed point in the extended complex plane. In this case, the
          transformation is conjugate to a translation
          @@math:128:df5518@@
Such transformations are called [[em]]parabolic transformations[[/em]].
3. Classification using the trace

Another useful classification arises by associating the transformation
          with the matrix
          @@math:129:c3d2bf@@

After normalizing so that @@math:130:c73dcc@@, the classification depends on the trace:
          @@math:131:57dd52@@
[[strong]]Elliptic:[[/strong]] @@math:132:c82e8f@@
[[strong]]Parabolic:[[/strong]] @@math:133:33ca44@@
[[strong]]Hyperbolic:[[/strong]] @@math:134:3c7fe0@@

This classification is particularly important in geometry and dynamical systems,
          where it describes the qualitative behavior of iterates of the transformation.

The following animation provides a visual illustration
          of the classification of Möbius transformations.
          Select the graphic view with mouse
          and then press the keys:

Classification of Möbius Transformations

A fundamental way to understand linear fractional (Möbius) transformations is through their fixed points. A point @@math:118:1c5ddf@@ is called a fixed point of a transformation @@math:119:e42f64@@ if @@math:120:306011@@ For a transformation @@math:121:09de4e@@ the fixed points are obtained by solving @@math:122:d4a359@@ which leads to the quadratic equation @@math:123:a556cb@@

Thus, a Möbius transformation has either one or two fixed points in the extended complex plane. This observation leads to a natural classification.

1. Transformations with two distinct fixed points

If the quadratic equation has two distinct solutions, then the transformation has two distinct fixed points. In this case, the transformation is conjugate to a simple dilation or rotation of the form @@math:124:e54486@@

  • If @@math:125:d1078e@@, the transformation is called a hyperbolic transformation.
  • If @@math:126:1311b4@@ (but @@math:127:23705c@@ to avoid the identity), the transformation is called an elliptic transformation.

Geometrically, these transformations preserve the set of circles and lines and act as scalings or rotations after a suitable change of coordinates.

2. Transformations with one (double) fixed point

If the quadratic equation has a repeated root, then the transformation has exactly one fixed point in the extended complex plane. In this case, the transformation is conjugate to a translation @@math:128:df5518@@

Such transformations are called parabolic transformations.

3. Classification using the trace

Another useful classification arises by associating the transformation with the matrix @@math:129:c3d2bf@@

After normalizing so that @@math:130:c73dcc@@, the classification depends on the trace: @@math:131:57dd52@@

  • Elliptic: @@math:132:c82e8f@@
  • Parabolic: @@math:133:33ca44@@
  • Hyperbolic: @@math:134:3c7fe0@@

This classification is particularly important in geometry and dynamical systems, where it describes the qualitative behavior of iterates of the transformation.

The following animation provides a visual illustration of the classification of Möbius transformations. Select the graphic view with mouse and then press the keys:
